Q1: I tried to keep a consistent study routine, usually studying in the evenings after my part-time work and other responsibilities. With limited resources at home, I had to be strategic about when I could access materials and focus properly. I'd typically block out about two hours most nights for reading and writing notes, spreading my 13 hours across the week as evenly as I could manage. It wasn't always perfect, but I made it work with what I had.
Q2: I aimed for regularity, but honestly, deadlines and my work schedule sometimes threw things off. I'd try to study at the same times each week, but when assignments were due or when I picked up extra shifts, everything shifted around. The weeks leading up to exams were definitely more intense and less predictable. I learned to be flexible because rigid schedules just weren't realistic for my situation.
Q3: Usually I logged in to check if there were new announcements or to download reading materials for the week. I also went on to see assignment details and deadlines since I couldn't afford to miss anything important. Sometimes I'd log in just to make sure I wasn't falling behind, especially when I felt that scared, anxious feeling creeping up about whether I was keeping pace with everyone else.
Q4: Yeah, after a few weeks it became automatic to log in first thing when I sat down to study. I'd open the LMS, check what was new, download what I needed, and then get to work. It was like checking my phone in the morning—just part of the routine. The consistency helped me feel more in control even when things felt overwhelming.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were most useful for me since I learn best through reading and writing. I'd download PDFs and lecture notes and really dig into them with my own annotations. The assignment rubrics were also incredibly helpful because they told me exactly what was expected. Videos were okay, but I got more out of text-based content that I could read at my own pace and highlight.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the discussion forums entirely—I never posted anything there. It's not that I didn't care, but I felt scared to put my thoughts out there publicly, and honestly, I learned better through reading than through online conversations. Some of the supplementary videos I'd skip if I felt I already understood the concept from the readings. Time was precious, so I had to prioritize.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to what I'd be assessed on or if it genuinely helped me understand difficult concepts. I needed to see the practical value because I couldn't waste hours on things that wouldn't help my grades. If something clicked and made the material clearer, that was time well spent. I also valued activities I could do offline since my internet access isn't always reliable.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I often couldn't afford. Required work always came first because I knew my grades depended on it. When I did have extra time, I'd sometimes look at optional materials, but there was always this pressure in the back of my mind about whether I should be focusing on something mandatory instead. Required activities had urgency; optional ones felt like nice-to-haves.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my studying happened offline with downloaded files and handwritten notes. I'd print readings when I could or save PDFs to my phone and laptop to study during commutes or when the internet was spotty. I also used textbooks from the library and made extensive written notes because that's how information sticks for me. A lot of my learning happened away from any screen.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd spend hours going through my printed notes, rewriting key concepts, and testing myself with flashcards I made by hand. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. I'd also study with a couple of peers in person at the library, working through problems together, but that peer interaction wasn't captured in the LMS at all. My best learning often happened completely offline.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture PDF or reading once, then spend days working through it without going back online. I'd annotate it, summarize sections in my notebook, and review it multiple times before moving on. The LMS would just show one download, but that single file might represent ten hours of actual study. It was more efficient for me to work that way given my situation.
Q12: There were definitely moments when fear and uncertainty made it harder to engage. Around midterms, I felt scared about whether I was doing enough, whether I'd actually pass. When my work schedule got heavy or when I was exhausted, the course felt like this mountain I couldn't climb. The mindfulness exercises helped a bit, but some weeks I just had to push through feeling like I was barely keeping my head above water.
Q13: Definitely. When I was tired from work or stressed about money, I'd log into the LMS but not really absorb anything. Competing responsibilities meant I sometimes had to choose between studying and earning, which was a constant tension. Fatigue made me less efficient, so I'd spend more time staring at materials without really processing them. Confusion about complex topics also slowed me down significantly.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd scroll through materials or watch videos but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd be worried about bills or exhausted from a long shift, and I'd realize I'd read three pages without retaining anything. Those sessions technically counted as engagement in the system, but mentally I wasn't there. I'd have to go back and redo that work later when I could actually focus.
Q15: I postponed some assignments even when I knew they mattered because I was scared of not doing them well enough. That fear of failure sometimes paralyzed me more than motivated me. I'd also delay difficult readings when I was mentally drained, telling myself I'd tackle them when I was fresher. It wasn't laziness—it was trying to protect myself from feeling overwhelmed, even though it sometimes backfired.
Q16: Deadlines were my main driver for LMS activity. I'd log in more frequently as due dates approached, checking requirements and submitting work. Without deadlines, I might have spread things out more evenly, but the pressure of a due date forced me to prioritize. Sometimes this meant cramming, which wasn't ideal, but it got the work done. Deadlines created structure when my life felt chaotic.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected my engagement. When I got positive comments on assignments, it boosted my confidence and made me want to engage more. Critical feedback sometimes scared me initially, but I'd use it to improve on the next assignment. I wish feedback came faster sometimes because waiting made me anxious about whether I was on the right track. Good feedback was like fuel for my motivation.
Q18: The course structure with clear weekly modules helped me stay organized despite everything else going on. Having a predictable rhythm meant I could plan around my work schedule and other commitments. The structure gave me anchor points throughout the semester. However, when multiple deadlines clustered together, it became overwhelming and I'd struggle to keep up with everything at once.
Q19: Having downloadable materials made engagement much easier for someone like me with limited reliable internet. The clear assignment rubrics also helped because I knew exactly what was expected. What made it harder was when participation was expected in real-time discussions—my schedule didn't always allow for that. Also, some video-heavy content was harder for me to engage with compared to written materials.
Q20: I think students might be searching for understanding, trying to figure things out, but struggling to produce the final work. Maybe they're reading and re-reading, watching videos multiple times, but can't translate that into completed assignments due to confusion or fear of failure. High activity could also mean they're anxious and checking obsessively without actually progressing. It doesn't necessarily mean they're not trying.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience somewhat. I performed reasonably well on assignments despite not showing tons of LMS activity because I did most of my work offline. My reading and writing happened with printed materials and notebooks. Someone looking at my login frequency might think I wasn't engaged, but I was putting in serious hours—just not in ways the system could see. Quality of engagement matters more than quantity of clicks.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when someone accessed materials and submitted work, which gives a basic picture of participation patterns. But they miss the depth of engagement—whether someone actually understood what they read, how much time they spent with offline materials, or what emotional state they were in while studying. They can't capture the fear, the determination, or the real learning that happens away from the screen.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material, trying to understand it deeply, and caring about doing well. It's not just showing up or clicking through content—it's being mentally present and invested in learning. For me, engagement also means persisting even when things are scary or difficult, finding ways to learn despite obstacles. True engagement is about commitment, not just activity.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I studied with printed materials and handwritten notes, or the peer study sessions at the library. It wouldn't capture the fear I felt about failing or the determination I had to succeed despite limited resources. It can't show that I was working a job while studying, or that I practiced mindfulness to manage my stress. The human struggle behind the data is completely invisible.
